command:
System instructions:

Plan a compact, faithful, formalization-sensitive proof graph for the user's theorem. Return only JSON that matches the supplied schema. In one pass, choose the graph shape and rank the best local formalization candidates. Keep the graph readable and preserve one or two strong formal islands when they carry real inferential load. Preserve the user's mathematical notation, especially LaTeX delimiters and formulas, whenever possible.

User task:

Theorem title hint: Untitled theorem

Theorem statement:
Weak maximum principle via the negative part.

Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a bounded smooth domain, and let \(u\) be a sufficiently regular function such that
\[
-\Delta u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega,
\]
and
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{on } \partial \Omega.
\]
Then
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega.
\]

Raw informal proof:
We argue by testing the differential inequality against the negative part of \(u\).

Define the negative part
\[
u_-(x)=\max\{-u(x),0\}.
\]
Then \(u_-(x)\ge 0\) everywhere, and \(u_-(x)=0\) exactly where \(u(x)\ge 0\). Since \(u\ge 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), the function \(u_-\) vanishes on the boundary.

Multiply the inequality
\[
-\Delta u \ge 0
\]
by \(u_-\) and integrate over \(\Omega\):
\[
\int_\Omega (-\Delta u)\,u_-\,dx \ge 0.
\]

Now integrate by parts. Because \(u_-=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), the boundary term vanishes, so
\[
\int_\Omega (-\Delta u)\,u_-\,dx
=
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla u_-\,dx.
\]
Therefore
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla u_-\,dx \ge 0.
\]

Next, we identify this integral more explicitly. On the set where \(u(x)\ge 0\), we have \(u_-(x)=0\), so \(\nabla u_-(x)=0\) there. On the set where \(u(x)<0\), we have
\[
u_-(x)=-u(x),
\]
so
\[
\nabla u_-(x)=-\nabla u(x).
\]
Hence on the set \(\{u<0\}\),
\[
\nabla u \cdot \nabla u_- = -|\nabla u_-|^2.
\]
Combining the two regions, we obtain
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u \cdot \nabla u_-\,dx
=
-\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx.
\]

Therefore
\[
-\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx \ge 0,
\]
which implies
\[
\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx \le 0.
\]
Since the integrand is nonnegative, we conclude that
\[
|\nabla u_-|^2=0
\]
almost everywhere, and hence \(u_-\) is constant in \(\Omega\). But \(u_-=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), so that constant must be zero. Thus
\[
u_-\equiv 0
\]
in \(\Omega\).

Finally, \(u_-\equiv 0\) means that \(u(x)\) is never negative. Therefore
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega.
\]

Return one JSON object with top-level keys theorem_title, theorem_statement, root_node_id, nodes, edges, and candidates.

The nodes and edges must follow the same informal extraction schema as before: nodes include id, title, informal_statement, informal_proof_text, and optional display_label; edges include source_id, target_id, and optional label and explanation.

The candidates array must include objects with node_id, priority, and rationale. Candidates should refer only to node ids that exist in the graph you emit.

Plan the graph and candidate ranking jointly. Optimize for a compact, faithful, formalization-sensitive graph with a small, high-yield candidate set.

A node should exist only if it is the root theorem, a nontrivial intermediate claim, a meaningful candidate formal island, a reused claim, a separate review obligation, or a conceptually distinct proof step that improves the final mixed informal/formal report.

Do not create separate nodes for bare assumptions, local variable setup, trivial restatements, goal-under-assumptions restatements, one-line substitutions, or duplicate near-equivalent claims.

When choosing graph granularity, preserve one or two local technical subclaims if they are concrete, inferentially important, plausibly formalizable, and substantially used by the surrounding proof. Good examples include concrete inequalities, integration-by-parts identities, explicit algebraic simplifications, monotonicity or concavity consequences, and concrete local estimates.

Keep the candidate set small. Prefer local, concrete, technically meaningful nodes that would reduce real human proof-checking burden. Disfavor easy side consequences, generic library facts, or claims that are much weaker than the surrounding local argument.

For a tiny proof, prefer a single root node unless a second node for a reusable or conceptually distinct lemma clearly improves the graph.

Preserve the original mathematical notation in your output whenever feasible. Do not normalize LaTeX into plain ASCII unless absolutely necessary.

{"theorem_title":"Untitled theorem","theorem_statement":"Weak maximum principle via the negative part.

Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a bounded smooth domain, and let \(u\) be a sufficiently regular function such that
\[
-\Delta u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega,
\]
and
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{on } \partial \Omega.
\]
Then
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega.
\]","root_node_id":"n0","nodes":[{"id":"n0","title":"Weak maximum principle via the negative part","informal_statement":"If \(-\Delta u \ge 0\) in \(\Omega\) and \(u \ge 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), then \(u \ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).","informal_proof_text":"Test the inequality against the negative part \(u_-(x)=\max\{-u(x),0\}\). Since \(u_-\) vanishes on \(\partial\Omega\), integration by parts gives \(\int_\Omega (-\Delta u)u_-\,dx=\int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-\,dx\ge 0\). On \(\{u<0\}\), \(\nabla u_-=-\nabla u\), while on \(\{u\ge 0\}\), \(\nabla u_-=0\), so \(\int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-\,dx=-\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx\). Hence \(\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx\le 0\), so \(u_-\) is constant; the boundary condition forces that constant to be zero. Therefore \(u_\equiv 0\), i.e. \(u\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).","display_label":"Weak maximum principle"},{"id":"n1","title":"Testing with the negative part and integration by parts","informal_statement":"For \(u_-(x)=\max\{-u(x),0\}\), one has \(u_-\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\), \(u_-=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), and
\[
\int_\Omega (-\Delta u)\,u_-\,dx = \int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-\,dx.
\]","informal_proof_text":"Because \(u\ge 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), the negative part \(u_-\) has zero boundary trace. Thus it is an admissible test function for the weak form of \(-\Delta u \ge 0\), and integration by parts removes the boundary term.","display_label":"Test by \(u_-\)"},{"id":"n2","title":"Energy identity for the negative part","informal_statement":"The gradients satisfy
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-\,dx = -\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx.
\]","informal_proof_text":"On \(\{u\ge 0\}\), one has \(u_-=0\) and hence \(\nabla u_-=0\). On \(\{u<0\}\), \(u_-=-u\), so \(\nabla u_-=-\nabla u\). Therefore \(\nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-=-|\nabla u_-|^2\) pointwise where \(u<0\), and the identity follows after integrating over \(\Omega\).","display_label":"Negative-part identity"},{"id":"n3","title":"Vanishing of the negative part","informal_statement":"If \(\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx\le 0\), then \(u_-=0\) in \(\Omega\), hence \(u\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).","informal_proof_text":"Since \(|\nabla u_-|^2\ge 0\), the integral inequality forces \(|\nabla u_-|^2=0\) almost everywhere. Thus \(u_-\) is constant on \(\Omega\); the boundary condition \(u_-=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\) forces that constant to be zero. Consequently \(u_-\equiv 0\), so \(u\) never takes negative values.","display_label":"Conclude \(u_-=0\)"}],"edges":[{"source_id":"n1","target_id":"n2","label":"compute","explanation":"The integration-by-parts identity is sharpened by identifying the integrand in terms of \(u_-\)."},{"source_id":"n2","target_id":"n3","label":"sign argument","explanation":"The identity yields a nonpositive integral of a nonnegative quantity, forcing \(\nabla u_- = 0\) a.e."},{"source_id":"n3","target_id":"n0","label":"final conclusion","explanation":"Once \(u_-\equiv 0\), the theorem follows immediately."}],"candidates":[{"node_id":"n2","priority":1,"rationale":"This is the main local formal island: the pointwise case split on \(u\ge 0\) vs. \(u<0\) produces the decisive energy identity \(\int \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_- = -\int |\nabla u_-|^2\)."},{"node_id":"n1","priority":2,"rationale":"This packages the admissibility of \(u_-\) as a test function and the boundary-term cancellation, which is a standard but formally delicate step in weak maximum principle proofs."},{"node_id":"n3","priority":3,"rationale":"This is the final deduction from the nonpositive energy integral to \(u_-\equiv 0\), combining nonnegativity, constancy, and the boundary trace condition."}]}

payload.theorem_statement:
Weak maximum principle via the negative part.

Let \(\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d\) be a bounded smooth domain, and let \(u\) be a sufficiently regular function such that
\[
-\Delta u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega,
\]
and
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{on } \partial \Omega.
\]
Then
\[
u \ge 0 \quad \text{in } \Omega.
\]

nodes.title: Weak maximum principle via the negative part
nodes.informal_statement: If \(-\Delta u \ge 0\) in \(\Omega\) and \(u \ge 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), then \(u \ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).
nodes.informal_proof_text:
Test the inequality against the negative part \(u_-(x)=\max\{-u(x),0\}\). Since \(u_-\) vanishes on \(\partial\Omega\), integration by parts gives \(\int_\Omega (-\Delta u)u_-\,dx=\int_\Omega 
abla u\cdot
abla u_-\,dx\ge 0\). On \(\{u<0\}\), \(
abla u_-=-
abla u\), while on \(\{u\ge 0\}\), \(
abla u_-=0\), so \(\int_\Omega 
abla u\cdot
abla u_-\,dx=-\int_\Omega |
abla u_-|^2\,dx\). Hence \(\int_\Omega |
abla u_-|^2\,dx\le 0\), so \(u_-\) is constant; the boundary condition forces that constant to be zero. Therefore \(u_\equiv 0\), i.e. \(u\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).

nodes.title: Testing with the negative part and integration by parts
nodes.informal_statement:
For \(u_-(x)=\max\{-u(x),0\}\), one has \(u_-\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\), \(u_-=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), and
\[
\int_\Omega (-\Delta u)\,u_-\,dx = \int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-\,dx.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text: Because \(u\ge 0\) on \(\partial\Omega\), the negative part \(u_-\) has zero boundary trace. Thus it is an admissible test function for the weak form of \(-\Delta u \ge 0\), and integration by parts removes the boundary term.

nodes.title: Energy identity for the negative part
nodes.informal_statement:
The gradients satisfy
\[
\int_\Omega \nabla u\cdot\nabla u_-\,dx = -\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx.
\]
nodes.informal_proof_text:
On \(\{u\ge 0\}\), one has \(u_-=0\) and hence \(
abla u_-=0\). On \(\{u<0\}\), \(u_-=-u\), so \(
abla u_-=-
abla u\). Therefore \(
abla u\cdot
abla u_-=-|
abla u_-|^2\) pointwise where \(u<0\), and the identity follows after integrating over \(\Omega\).

nodes.title: Vanishing of the negative part
nodes.informal_statement: If \(\int_\Omega |\nabla u_-|^2\,dx\le 0\), then \(u_-=0\) in \(\Omega\), hence \(u\ge 0\) in \(\Omega\).
nodes.informal_proof_text: Since \(|\nabla u_-|^2\ge 0\), the integral inequality forces \(|\nabla u_-|^2=0\) almost everywhere. Thus \(u_-\) is constant on \(\Omega\); the boundary condition \(u_-=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\) forces that constant to be zero. Consequently \(u_-\equiv 0\), so \(u\) never takes negative values.

edges.label: compute
edges.explanation: The integration-by-parts identity is sharpened by identifying the integrand in terms of \(u_-\).

edges.label: sign argument
edges.explanation: The identity yields a nonpositive integral of a nonnegative quantity, forcing \(\nabla u_- = 0\) a.e.

candidates.rationale:
This is the main local formal island: the pointwise case split on \(u\ge 0\) vs. \(u<0\) produces the decisive energy identity \(\int 
abla u\cdot
abla u_- = -\int |
abla u_-|^2\).
This packages the admissibility of \(u_-\) as a test function and the boundary-term cancellation, which is a standard but formally delicate step in weak maximum principle proofs.
This is the final deduction from the nonpositive energy integral to \(u_-\equiv 0\), combining nonnegativity, constancy, and the boundary trace condition.
